title: Tesla Urges Trump Administration to Keep Vehicle Emissions Rules, Climate Finding
content:
Tesla has urged the Trump administration to abandon plans to repeal U.S. vehicle emissions standards and the governmentâ€™s longstanding finding that greenhouse gases pose a danger to public health. In formal comments, the company argued that the Environmental Protection Agencyâ€™s (EPA) proposal would allow automakers to bypass requirements for measuring, controlling, and reporting emissions from highway vehicles. Tesla said such a rollback would undermine incentives that support electric vehicle (EV) innovation and investment. The appeal comes in contrast to the stance taken by General Motors, Toyota, Volkswagen, and nearly all major automakers, who earlier this week asked the EPA to scale back emissions limits that require steadily higher volumes of EV production. Teslaâ€™s position is complicated by its heavy reliance on regulatory credits for revenue. In 2024, the company earned $2.8 billion globally by selling zero-emission credits to automakers that needed them to comply with emissions rules. Tesla warned that dismantling federal green vehicle regulations would sharply reduce the value of such credits.
Tesla also noted past investments in compliance measures, including spending $25 per vehicle since 2019 on environmentally friendly air-conditioning systems, which accumulated to tens of millions of dollars across its fleet. The company argued that dismantling emissions standards would unfairly diminish the payoff from these investments.
The debate is unfolding amid broader political tensions. Senator Bernie Moreno has criticized the compliance costs that other automakers pay Tesla, calling them â€œoutrageous.â€ Tesla CEO Elon Musk has also had a strained relationship with President Trump, particularly after Musk opposed a budget bill that eliminated consumer EV tax credits expiring this September. The administration has already announced that automakers will not face penalties for failing to meet fuel economy rules since 2022 and has blocked Californiaâ€™s plan to phase out gasoline-only cars by 2035.
author: Analysts Account
